title: Chapter 28: The Signal
content: Lira came to them.

Not at the Night Market—too public. She appeared at the northwest junction three days early, out of breath, her immaculate jacket scuffed with tunnel grime. The composure had cracked. What remained underneath was fear.
"We need to talk," she said. "Now.”
Ash blocked her path. "You don't come down here. That was the agreement."
"The agreement is irrelevant. You need to hear this."
Wren stepped forward. "Let her through."
Ash's jaw tightened, but he moved. Lira entered the junction chamber, pressed her back against the wall, and spoke in clipped sentences that fell like shrapnel.
"NeuroScale is deploying a countermeasure. Project name: Cleaner Protocol. AI-driven surveillance units designed to identify, track, and contain data anomalies in urban populations."
"Data anomalies," Ash said flatly.

"That's what they call you. Signatures that don't match the social graph. Biometrics that flicker. People the system can't process." Lira's hands were shaking; she pressed them flat against her thighs. "The Cleaner Protocol is built to find you. All of you. And when it does—"
She didn't finish.
Wren's stomach dropped. "What happens when it does?"

"Containment. Rerouting. Research." Lira's voice went clinical—a defense mechanism, parroting the language she'd learned in boardrooms. "Subjects will be transported to a secure facility for longitudinal study. The protocol includes provisions for memory modification and social reinstatement—meaning they'll erase what you know and put you back into the system as someone else. Someone docile. Someone who doesn't remember being erased."
The words hung in the air like smoke after an explosion.

Boz, who'd been listening from the corridor, let out a low curse. Mira whimpered somewhere in the dark. Kade and June stood frozen near the entrance, the teenager's hand gripping her brother's sleeve.
"When?" Ash asked.
"Two weeks. The deployment window is fourteen days. After that, the Hounds go live."
"Hounds?"

"Proto-Hounds. That's what the development team calls them. AI units with physical enforcement capability—modified security drones, retooled for anomaly detection. They'll sweep the city in grid patterns. Anything that doesn't resolve in the social database gets flagged for pickup."
Ash turned away. His tattoos flared—bright, angry, pulsing with the rhythm of a war drum only he could hear.
"Two weeks," he repeated.

"That's not all." Lira reached into her bag and pulled out a data drive—small, matte black, unmarked. "I pulled everything I could access. Internal memos, deployment timelines, the Hound specifications. But there's something else. Something buried in the project files."
She held the drive out.
"A reference to something called the Signal Key."
Wren took the drive. Turned it over in her fingers. The plastic was cold and smooth.
"What's the Signal Key?"

"I don't know exactly. The references are oblique—code names and fragments. But from what I can piece together, it's a device. Hardware, not software. Something Vance developed during the original ANX-00 trials. A counter-frequency that can theoretically reverse the perceptual dissociation."
"Reverse it," Wren said slowly. "You mean—make us visible again?"

"That's what the files imply. The Signal Key generates a pulse that re-stabilizes the neural pathways ANX-00 disrupted. If it works—if it even exists—it could undo what the drug did. Bring you back into the social graph. Make the Hounds useless, because you'd no longer be anomalies."
The chamber went silent.
The possibility hung there, enormous and fragile. Visibility. Return. The restoration of everything the invisibles had lost.
Wren's hands tightened around the drive.
"Where is it?"
"I don't know. The files reference a secure facility—NeuroScale's research campus, north district. But the specifics are redacted at a clearance level I never reached."
"So it might not exist."

"It might not." Lira met her gaze. "But Vance built it. I'm sure of that. She wouldn't have referenced something that didn't exist in a document she thought nobody would ever read. The Signal Key is real. The question is whether we can get to it before the Hounds get to us."
Ash moved.

He crossed the chamber in three strides and planted himself between Wren and Lira, facing the group that had gathered. His tattoos blazed—not the soft ambient glow Wren had grown accustomed to, but a fierce, sustained burn that painted the walls in shifting blue light.
"We don't need the Signal Key," he said.
Wren stared at him. "What?"
"Two weeks. That's not enough time to infiltrate a secure facility, locate a device we're not even sure exists, and extract it. That's a fantasy. What we need is to hit them first."
"Hit them?"

"NeuroScale. The headquarters. The servers. The research campus. Everything." His voice was rising, the controlled menace giving way to something rawer. "If we destroy their data, their infrastructure, their capacity to deploy the Hounds, the Cleaner Protocol dies before it launches. No Hounds. No problem."
"You want to go to war," Boz said quietly.
"I want to survive. Same thing."
"No." Wren stepped forward. "It's not the same thing."
Ash turned. The glow of his tattoos threw hard shadows across his face.
"Explain."
"War means casualties. War means exposure. You start attacking NeuroScale infrastructure, you draw attention. You give them a reason to accelerate the Hounds, not stop them."
"I give them a reason to be afraid. Fear works."

"Fear works both ways." She could hear her own voice rising to match his. "You blow up a building, they don't reconsider—they retaliate. They crack down harder. And meanwhile, we're fighting instead of finding the one thing that could make the fight unnecessary."
"The Signal Key might not exist."

"The Hounds might not work either. We're guessing either way. But at least the Key gives us a chance to solve the actual problem—the invisibility. If we're visible, the Hounds can't touch us. We become people again. People with rights. People who can fight back in the open, from a position of strength, not from a hole in the ground."
Ash's jaw worked. The tattoos pulsed faster.
"And if the Key doesn't exist? If we spend two weeks chasing a ghost while NeuroScale deploys the Hounds?"
"Then we'll know. And we'll have lost nothing but time we would have spent anyway running and hiding."
"We'd have lost the element of surprise."
"We'd have gained each other."
The words landed differently than she intended.
Ash stared at her. The gathered invisibles stared at her. Lira, still pressed against the wall, watched with an expression Wren couldn't read—something between calculation and curiosity.
"What does that mean?" Ash asked.
"It means—" Wren took a breath. "It means you want revenge. I understand that. They killed you. They made you into this. You have every right to want them to pay."
"But."

"But if we go after them now, with two weeks' notice and an entire community to protect, we're not strategizing. We're reacting. We're doing what they expect invisible people to do—lashing out from the margins. Staying ghosts."
She held up the drive.
"The Signal Key is a chance to stop being ghosts. To come back. To make them see us—not because we forced them to look, but because they can't look away. Don't you want that?"
Ash's silence was a living thing. It filled the chamber, pressing against the walls.
When he finally spoke, his voice was barely audible.
"I don't know how to want anything else."
The admission cut deeper than any argument.

Wren saw it then—the full shape of what three years had done to him. Revenge wasn't a strategy. It was an identity. The list, the nightly raids, the systematic destruction—it was the only way he knew to exist. Without it, he was just a dead man in a tunnel with nowhere to go and no one to be.
The Signal Key threatened all of that. If they became visible, Ash would have to figure out who he was without the war. And that terrified him more than the Hounds ever could.
She stepped closer. Close enough that only he could hear.
"I know," she said. "But you're going to have to learn."
He didn't respond. But he didn't argue either.
The meeting broke apart in fragments—Boz and the others dispersing into the tunnels, Lira vanishing the way she'd come, the drive changing hands twice before it ended up back in Wren's pocket.
Two weeks.
Wren stood in the junction chamber alone, turning the drive over and over in her fingers. The plastic had warmed to her body temperature. It felt almost alive.
Two weeks until the Hounds. Two weeks to find a device that might not exist in a facility she couldn't enter.
Fourteen days to choose between a war they might lose and a cure they might never find.
She pressed the drive against her sternum and thought about the Glitch Garden. The mural. The door she'd drawn—half-open, light spilling through.
It was time to walk through it.
She just hoped there was something on the other side.
